main_paper.raw: {"methods":["E(n)-Equivariant Topological Neural Networks (ETNNs): E(n)-equivariant message-passing networks operating on combinatorial complexes, generalizing graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial, path, and cell complexes","Scalarization method for incorporating geometric invariants and learnable updates of geometric features in message passing","General framework for deriving E(n)-equivariant variants of topological deep learning (TDL) models from ETNNs"],"problems":["Modeling higher-order and multi-way interactions in data, which are not well captured by standard graph neural networks (GNNs)","Incorporating geometric features (positions, velocities) into topological deep learning models while respecting E(n) symmetries (rotation, reflection, translation equivariance)","Efficiently learning from richly structured data with heterogeneous interactions"],"datasets":["QM9 benchmark (molecular property prediction)","Multi-resolution irregular geospatial data for land-use regression (hyper-local air pollution estimation)"],"metrics":["Prediction accuracy (for molecular property prediction)","Memory usage","Runtime per epoch"],"results":[{"metric":"Prediction accuracy (QM9)","value":"ETNNs match or surpass state-of-the-art equivariant TDL models (e.g., EMPSNs)"},{"metric":"Memory usage","value":"ETNNs use less than half the memory of EMPSNs"},{"metric":"Runtime per epoch","value":"ETNNs require almost half the runtime per epoch compared to EMPSNs"}],"novelty_claims":["First general framework for E(n)-equivariant message passing over combinatorial complexes, unifying and generalizing previous approaches for graphs, simplicial complexes, and hypergraphs","ETNNs natively incorporate geometric node features while respecting E(n) equivariance, enabling principled geometric inductive bias in TDL models","ETNNs are more expressive than previous scalarization-based E(n)-equivariant GNNs, able to distinguish more complex geometric graph structures","ETNNs achieve state-of-the-art or better performance with significantly reduced computational resources (memory and runtime)","Demonstration of ETNNs' applicability to both molecular property prediction and a new challenging geospatial benchmark for TDL"]}

methods: E(n)-Equivariant Topological Neural Networks (ETNNs): E(n)-equivariant message-passing networks operating on combinatorial complexes, generalizing graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial, path, and cell complexes
Scalarization method for incorporating geometric invariants and learnable updates of geometric features in message passing
General framework for deriving E(n)-equivariant variants of topological deep learning (TDL) models from ETNNs
problems: Modeling higher-order and multi-way interactions in data, which are not well captured by standard graph neural networks (GNNs)
Incorporating geometric features (positions, velocities) into topological deep learning models while respecting E(n) symmetries (rotation, reflection, translation equivariance)
Efficiently learning from richly structured data with heterogeneous interactions
datasets: Multi-resolution irregular geospatial data for land-use regression (hyper-local air pollution estimation)

results.value: ETNNs match or surpass state-of-the-art equivariant TDL models (e.g., EMPSNs)
ETNNs use less than half the memory of EMPSNs
ETNNs require almost half the runtime per epoch compared to EMPSNs

novelty_claims: First general framework for E(n)-equivariant message passing over combinatorial complexes, unifying and generalizing previous approaches for graphs, simplicial complexes, and hypergraphs
ETNNs natively incorporate geometric node features while respecting E(n) equivariance, enabling principled geometric inductive bias in TDL models
ETNNs are more expressive than previous scalarization-based E(n)-equivariant GNNs, able to distinguish more complex geometric graph structures
ETNNs achieve state-of-the-art or better performance with significantly reduced computational resources (memory and runtime)
Demonstration of ETNNs' applicability to both molecular property prediction and a new challenging geospatial benchmark for TDL

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Introduction of a novel notion of higher-order topological directionality based on directed simplicial paths","Design of Directed Simplicial Neural Networks (Dir-SNNs), a message-passing neural network operating on directed simplicial complexes"],"problems":["Lack of models in Topological Deep Learning (TDL) that account for higher-order directionality in combinatorial topological spaces","Inability of existing TDL models to leverage asymmetric and directed interactions among higher-order simplices","Need for more expressive models to distinguish isomorphic directed graphs"],"datasets":["Synthetic dataset for source localization task on directed and undirected simplicial complexes"],"metrics":["Performance on source localization task (e.g., accuracy or similar task-specific metric)","Expressiveness in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs"],"results":[{"metric":"Source localization performance (directed complex)","value":"Dir-SNNs outperform undirected SNNs"},{"metric":"Source localization performance (undirected complex)","value":"Dir-SNNs perform comparably to undirected SNNs"},{"metric":"Expressiveness in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs","value":"Dir-SNNs are more expressive than directed GNNs"}],"novelty_claims":["First introduction of a formal notion of higher-order topological directionality for simplicial complexes","First Topological Deep Learning model (Dir-SNN) that leverages higher-order directionality in message passing","Theoretical and empirical demonstration that Dir-SNNs are more expressive than directed graph neural networks in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs"]}

methods: Introduction of a novel notion of higher-order topological directionality based on directed simplicial paths
Design of Directed Simplicial Neural Networks (Dir-SNNs), a message-passing neural network operating on directed simplicial complexes
problems: Lack of models in Topological Deep Learning (TDL) that account for higher-order directionality in combinatorial topological spaces
Inability of existing TDL models to leverage asymmetric and directed interactions among higher-order simplices
Need for more expressive models to distinguish isomorphic directed graphs
datasets: Synthetic dataset for source localization task on directed and undirected simplicial complexes
metrics: Performance on source localization task (e.g., accuracy or similar task-specific metric)
Expressiveness in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs

results.value: Dir-SNNs outperform undirected SNNs
Dir-SNNs perform comparably to undirected SNNs

results.metric: Expressiveness in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs
results.value: Dir-SNNs are more expressive than directed GNNs

novelty_claims: First introduction of a formal notion of higher-order topological directionality for simplicial complexes
First Topological Deep Learning model (Dir-SNN) that leverages higher-order directionality in message passing
Theoretical and empirical demonstration that Dir-SNNs are more expressive than directed graph neural networks in distinguishing isomorphic directed graphs

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["VN-EGNN: E(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Network with Virtual Nodes and extended message passing scheme for protein binding site identification","Multi-modal objective combining node-level binary classification (semantic segmentation) and direct prediction of binding site center coordinates"],"problems":["Accurate identification of protein binding sites (binding pocket centers) from 3D protein structures","Limitations of current GNN-based methods for binding site identification, including lack of dedicated nodes for binding pockets and oversquashing in message passing"],"datasets":["COACH420","HOLO4K","PDBbind2020"],"metrics":["Distance between predicted and observed binding site centers","Node-level binary classification accuracy (semantic segmentation)"],"results":[{"metric":"State-of-the-art performance on binding site center localization","value":"VN-EGNN outperforms previous methods on COACH420, HOLO4K, and PDBbind2020 (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["First E(3)-equivariant GNN architecture using virtual nodes for protein binding site identification","Virtual nodes in EGNNs enable learning of useful neural representations and accurate prediction of binding site centers without relying on a priori knowledge","Extended message passing scheme with virtual nodes alleviates oversquashing and improves predictive performance for binding site identification"]}

methods: VN-EGNN: E(3)-Equivariant Graph Neural Network with Virtual Nodes and extended message passing scheme for protein binding site identification
Multi-modal objective combining node-level binary classification (semantic segmentation) and direct prediction of binding site center coordinates
problems: Accurate identification of protein binding sites (binding pocket centers) from 3D protein structures
Limitations of current GNN-based methods for binding site identification, including lack of dedicated nodes for binding pockets and oversquashing in message passing
metrics: Distance between predicted and observed binding site centers

results.metric: State-of-the-art performance on binding site center localization
results.value: VN-EGNN outperforms previous methods on COACH420, HOLO4K, and PDBbind2020 (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: First E(3)-equivariant GNN architecture using virtual nodes for protein binding site identification
Virtual nodes in EGNNs enable learning of useful neural representations and accurate prediction of binding site centers without relying on a priori knowledge
Extended message passing scheme with virtual nodes alleviates oversquashing and improves predictive performance for binding site identification

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["E(n) Equivariant Message Passing Simplicial Networks (EMPSNs)","Generalization of E(n) Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (EGNNs) to simplicial complexes","Incorporation of E(n) invariant geometric information in message passing inspired by DimeNet-like architectures"],"problems":["Learning on geometric graphs and point clouds with equivariance to rotations, translations, and reflections","Expressivity limitations of standard Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) in capturing higher-dimensional graph structures","Combating over-smoothing in message passing networks, especially on high-dimensional simplicial structures"],"datasets":["N-body experiment","QM9"],"metrics":["Performance (accuracy or error, as compared to baselines)","Forward time (computational efficiency)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance improvement over EGNNs and MPSNs","value":"General increase in performance without requiring more parameters"},{"metric":"Competitiveness with state-of-the-art on N-body and QM9","value":"On par with state-of-the-art approaches"},{"metric":"Forward time","value":"No significant increase compared to other existing approaches"}],"novelty_claims":["First E(n) equivariant formulation of Simplicial Message Passing Networks, combining geometric and topological graph approaches","Generalization of EGNNs to learn features on simplicial complexes, incorporating more E(n) invariant information","Demonstration that higher-dimensional simplex learning improves performance even with few higher-dimensional simplices, without increasing parameter count","Evidence that incorporating geometric information helps combat over-smoothing in graph networks across all dimensions"]}

methods: E(n) Equivariant Message Passing Simplicial Networks (EMPSNs)
Generalization of E(n) Equivariant Graph Neural Networks (EGNNs) to simplicial complexes
Incorporation of E(n) invariant geometric information in message passing inspired by DimeNet-like architectures
problems: Learning on geometric graphs and point clouds with equivariance to rotations, translations, and reflections
Expressivity limitations of standard Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) in capturing higher-dimensional graph structures
Combating over-smoothing in message passing networks, especially on high-dimensional simplicial structures
metrics: Performance (accuracy or error, as compared to baselines)

results.metric: Performance improvement over EGNNs and MPSNs
results.value: General increase in performance without requiring more parameters

results.metric: Competitiveness with state-of-the-art on N-body and QM9
results.value: On par with state-of-the-art approaches
No significant increase compared to other existing approaches

novelty_claims: First E(n) equivariant formulation of Simplicial Message Passing Networks, combining geometric and topological graph approaches
Generalization of EGNNs to learn features on simplicial complexes, incorporating more E(n) invariant information
Demonstration that higher-dimensional simplex learning improves performance even with few higher-dimensional simplices, without increasing parameter count
Evidence that incorporating geometric information helps combat over-smoothing in graph networks across all dimensions

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Generalized Simplicial Attention Neural Networks (GSANs)","Masked self-attention mechanisms for simplicial complexes","GSAN-Joint (low-complexity variant with constrained weight-sharing)","GSAN-Hodge (variant leveraging weighted Dirac operator and Hodge Laplacian structure)"],"problems":["Learning from data with multi-way (higher-order) interactions not captured by standard graphs","Processing and learning on data defined over simplicial complexes (higher-order topological spaces)","Joint processing of data associated with simplices of various orders (nodes, edges, triangles, etc.)","Improving performance on tasks such as trajectory prediction, missing data imputation, graph classification, and simplex prediction"],"datasets":["Ocean drifters tracks data (for trajectory prediction)","Citation complexes (for missing data imputation and simplex prediction)","Molecular graphs (for graph classification)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Imputation error","Prediction accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of GSAN, a novel neural network architecture for data on simplicial complexes using masked self-attention and Dirac operator-based weight sharing.","GSAN enables joint processing of data on different simplex orders via a principled, theoretically justified, and computationally efficient mechanism.","Design of a sparse projection operator to process the harmonic component of the data, capturing topological invariants.","Theoretical proof that GSANs are permutation-equivariant and simplicial-aware, recognizing and leveraging topological properties.","Introduction of two new GSAN variants: GSAN-Joint (low-complexity) and GSAN-Hodge (enforcing Hodge Laplacian structure).","GSANs outperform or compare favorably to existing graph and simplicial neural network models on several benchmark tasks."]}

methods: Generalized Simplicial Attention Neural Networks (GSANs)
Masked self-attention mechanisms for simplicial complexes
GSAN-Joint (low-complexity variant with constrained weight-sharing)
GSAN-Hodge (variant leveraging weighted Dirac operator and Hodge Laplacian structure)
problems: Learning from data with multi-way (higher-order) interactions not captured by standard graphs
Processing and learning on data defined over simplicial complexes (higher-order topological spaces)
Joint processing of data associated with simplices of various orders (nodes, edges, triangles, etc.)
Improving performance on tasks such as trajectory prediction, missing data imputation, graph classification, and simplex prediction
datasets: Ocean drifters tracks data (for trajectory prediction)
Citation complexes (for missing data imputation and simplex prediction)
novelty_claims: Introduction of GSAN, a novel neural network architecture for data on simplicial complexes using masked self-attention and Dirac operator-based weight sharing.
GSAN enables joint processing of data on different simplex orders via a principled, theoretically justified, and computationally efficient mechanism.
Design of a sparse projection operator to process the harmonic component of the data, capturing topological invariants.
Theoretical proof that GSANs are permutation-equivariant and simplicial-aware, recognizing and leveraging topological properties.
Introduction of two new GSAN variants: GSAN-Joint (low-complexity) and GSAN-Hodge (enforcing Hodge Laplacian structure).
GSANs outperform or compare favorably to existing graph and simplicial neural network models on several benchmark tasks.

methods: Introduction of combinatorial complexes (CCs) as a new topological domain generalizing graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial and cell complexes

problems: Lack of a unifying framework for topological deep learning (TDL) across various topological domains (graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial and cell complexes)

novelty_claims: Introduction of combinatorial complexes (CCs), a novel topological domain that unifies and generalizes graphs, hypergraphs, simplicial and cell complexes
Development of combinatorial complex neural networks (CCNNs), a general and unifying class of higher-order message-passing neural networks for topological deep learning
Definition of a push-forward operation enabling higher-order message passing and (un)pooling on CCs
Formalization of permutation and orientation equivariance for CCNNs

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Simplicial Attention Networks (SAT)","Signed attention mechanism for orientation equivariance"],"problems":["Learning on higher-order structures in graphs (simplicial complexes)","Generalisation of neural networks to unseen simplicial structures","Image classification on superpixel graphs","Trajectory classification on oriented simplicial complexes"],"datasets":["Superpixel graph dataset for image classification","Trajectory classification benchmark involving oriented simplicial complexes"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Simplicial Attention Networks (SAT), a new type of simplicial network that uses attention mechanisms to dynamically weigh interactions between neighbouring simplices.","Introduces a signed attention mechanism to make SAT orientation equivariant, which is desirable for models on (co)chain complexes.","SAT generalises Graph Attention Networks (GAT) to higher-order simplicial complexes.","SAT can adapt to novel structures and is not strictly tied to the combinatorial structure of the complex.","Demonstrates that SAT outperforms existing convolutional SNNs and GNNs on image and trajectory classification tasks."]}

methods: Simplicial Attention Networks (SAT)
Signed attention mechanism for orientation equivariance
problems: Learning on higher-order structures in graphs (simplicial complexes)
Generalisation of neural networks to unseen simplicial structures
Image classification on superpixel graphs
Trajectory classification on oriented simplicial complexes
datasets: Superpixel graph dataset for image classification
Trajectory classification benchmark involving oriented simplicial complexes
novelty_claims: Proposes Simplicial Attention Networks (SAT), a new type of simplicial network that uses attention mechanisms to dynamically weigh interactions between neighbouring simplices.
Introduces a signed attention mechanism to make SAT orientation equivariant, which is desirable for models on (co)chain complexes.
SAT generalises Graph Attention Networks (GAT) to higher-order simplicial complexes.
SAT can adapt to novel structures and is not strictly tied to the combinatorial structure of the complex.
Demonstrates that SAT outperforms existing convolutional SNNs and GNNs on image and trajectory classification tasks.

novelty_claims: Introduction of Message Passing Simplicial Networks (MPSNs), a message passing neural architecture for simplicial complexes, generalizing GNNs to higher-order structures.
Development of the Simplicial Weisfeiler-Lehman (SWL) test for distinguishing non-isomorphic simplicial complexes, and theoretical analysis relating its power to the 3-WL test.
Proof that MPSNs are strictly more powerful than the standard WL test and at least as powerful as the 3-WL test for distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs and complexes.
Analysis and proof that MPSNs have higher functional complexity (number of linear regions) than GNNs and SCNNs.
Empirical demonstration that MPSNs can distinguish challenging graph structures and improve classification accuracy on oriented simplicial complexes compared to GNNs.
